text:
US flagbearer Coco Gauff claimed she was ‘getting cheated’ by poor decisions as she was dumped out of the women’s singles competition at theParisOlympics with a 7-6 6-2 defeat to Donna Vekic.

In this afternoon’s tightly contested third-round clash at Roland-Garros, Vekic had a golden opportunity to pull away, with the Croatian leading 40-30 on Gauff’s serve in the second set – and the point proved hugely contentious.

It came after Gauff, the reigning US Open champion, had managed to throw away a 5-2 advantage in a dramatic first set.

As Gauff, trailing 3-2, moved backwards to hit a backhand near the baseline, the linesperson appeared to call the ball out before the American had swung her racket, causing her to miss.

However, the chair umpire, Jaume Campistol, took a different view, overruling the original decision before awarding the point to Vekic, putting the 28-year-old just two games away from victory.

A heated discussion then ensued between Gauff and Campistol, which resulted in the world number two breaking down in tears and accusing the officials of ‘cheating’ her out of a potential victory.

But Campistol held firm on his decision and Vekic – a semi-finalist at Wimbledon earlier this summer – went on to wrap up the win in emphatic fashion to secure a place in the quarter-finals.

GAUFF

‘I was going back to hit the ball and he called it out before I hit it.’

UMPIRE

‘You understand that I have to take a decision and in this case…’

GAUFF

‘But you have to be sure…’

UMPIRE

‘I am 100 per cent sure in this case.’

GAUFF

‘You’re not, you just said maybe I’m wrong!’

UMPIRE

‘Based on your reaction I might be wrong.’

GAUFF

‘I never argue these calls but he called it out before I hit the ball. It always happens to me on this court!

‘Call the supervisor. It always happens to me, every time. You understand that I always have to advocate for myself, all the time.

‘I’m getting cheated in this game. You guys are not fair to me.’

Croatian No.13 seed Vekic saw off Italy’s Lucia Bronzetti and Canada’s Bianca Andreescu to secure her place in the third round against Gauff.

Gauff must now turn her attentions to the first round of the mixed doubles competition, in which she is representing the United States alongside Taylor Fritz.

The American duo begin their campaign with a potentially tricky first-round tie against Maximo Gonzalez and Nadia Podoroska of Argentina.

Gauff is also due to compete in the women’s doubles competition with Jessica Pegula, with the pair handed a first-round match against Australia’s Daria Saville and Ellen Perez.

An emotional Gauff called on tennis authorities to take inspiration from football and introduce a ‘VAR system’ as she addressed the contentious call shortly after her defeat to Vekic.

‘I felt that they called it before I hit. I don’t think the ref disagreed. I think he just thought it didn’t, like, affect my swing, which I felt like it did,’ Gauff told The Athletic.

‘There have been multiple times this year where that happened to me where I feel like I always have to be an advocate for myself on the court.

‘I feel like in tennis, we should have a VAR system because these points are big deals.

‘Usually, afterwards they apologise. It’s kind of frustrating when the sorry doesn’t help you once the match is over. It’s not fair at all.’

title: Coco Gauff bursts into tears in total meltdown as she crashes out of Paris Olympics | Metro News
description: Coco Gauff claimed she was being 'cheated' by poor decisions as she was dumped out of the women's singles competition at the Paris Olympics.
